内容:
Welcome, Weekenders!
In this newsletter:
•
Tech Culture:
Billionaire tax
brings new boom time
to Tahoe haven
•
Plus, Recommendations—our weekly pop culture picks:
“
The Interview: What Does Tucker Carlson Really Believe? I Went to Maine to Find Out
,” “
Hated by All the Right People
,” and “
Widow’s Bay
”
A very old proverb
comes to mind when I think about this exact period in AI—because, really, it’s a moment when the enemy of your enemy can be your compute provider.
I’m referencing, of course, Elon Musk’s decision
to strike a deal
with Anthropic and sell it access to his data center campus in Memphis, Colossus 1. Until this week, Musk had seemed to view Anthropic strictly as a bitter rival to his xAI, which he recently folded into SpaceX; now the two companies are business partners. (Honestly, the AI industry treats labels and relationships much as a Fire Island share house does: It’s all very fluid.) Musk
said
he met with the team at Anthropic last week and decided to do the deal when “no one set off my evil detector.”
It’s a dramatic change in tone for Musk, who’d been merrily calling the startup “Misanthropic” as often as he could for several months. Perhaps he just misunderstood the word’s definition? No, it’s a matter of timing.
Anthropic’s Claude Code and Cowork products
have become enormous hits
, but the startup has faced a problem in securing the compute needed to keep up with demand. Having just really caught everyone’s attention after several years in the shadow of Google and OpenAI, Anthropic absolutely doesn’t want to slow down. It wants the compute as immediately as possible.
Meanwhile, Musk is incentivized to sell off Colossus access at this very moment in a way he wasn’t even a few months ago.
The Anthropic deal helps improve the financial situation for his SpaceX conglomerate as it prepares
for an IPO
next month. In addition, Musk got Anthropic to express interest in orbital data centers in
its announcement
of the Colossus deal; presumably, he’ll  point to Anthropic’s interest when he talks up outer space data centers on his IPO road show, even if they remain a totally unproven concept, like Bigfoot.
I wonder what the Anthropic tie-up means for the deals Musk did just a few weeks ago—when he sold compute to Cursor, a top Anthropic rival, and said he’d
acquired an option
to buy the startup or fork over a $10 billion break-up fee. There’s a very good chance we’re a few months away from Musk suing to get out of paying that fee, especially if Anthropic continues on its recent pace and seems to dim Cursor’s prospects further, making the startup less attractive to Musk.
Another factor at play: Don’t underestimate how much motivation Musk may be drawing from the chance to screw over Sam Altman at an instant when their relationship has been put on full public display.
In the court case between the two over OpenAI, Musk’s lawyers have been trying their best to shove their fingers in Altman’s eye for the last two weeks. But they
haven’t gotten close
to securing a decisive victory, which must irritate Musk, who has publicly made Altman a total bête noire.
So with Anthropic, Musk gets another opportunity to kick the shins of one enemy by locking arms with another, which reminds me of another adage: With friends like these…
What else from this week…
• I generally try to take what political operatives say with a heap of salt, but when they tell NPR they’re
rampantly using prediction markets
to trade off their own campaigns’ internal information, I totally believe it.
• Hunters and fishers think technology
has taken all the fun
out of hunting and fishing.
• Vibe change alert! Ezra Klein
has an argument
that the AI job-pocalypse won’t come to pass.
• DeepMind
has invested
in Fenris Creations (formerly CCP Games), the Iceland-based maker of Eve Online, a venerable internet strategy game that involves empire building in outer space. The game “requires skills that AI has not yet fully mastered,” said DeepMind executive. Oh, great—once the AIs are done conquering this planet, we’ll have already taught them how to take over other ones, too.
• According to The Atlantic, the IRS
once ran
a “secret AI fight club.”
• Quirky
doesn’t quite describe
Corgi, an AI insurance startup, which was recently valued at over $1 billion and has seen annualized revenue top $100 million: It operates the all-night Corgi Cafe, an honest-to-goodness coffee joint, in San Francisco’s Financial District. Most conference rooms at its office have mattresses shoved in a corner—since many of the young employees simply choose to crash overnight rather than go home. And the staff share responsibilities for Trudy, the office corgi, managing the tasks via Telegram bot.
• The Met Gala has been attracting a who’s who of Silicon Valley for years, but at the most recent one, the traditional celebrities took the opportunity
to express a strong anti-AI sentiment
. It was incredibly blatant—Katy Perry–in-an-Optimus-mask
blatant
—and it’s the kind of very public snub I’m sure has in turn got the industry elite turning up the heat on their policy and publicist teams.
—Abram Brown (
abe@theinformation.com
) .
Weekend’s Latest Stories
Tech Culture
Billionaire Tax Refugees Flock to Ritzy Nevada Lake Town
Incline Village at Lake Tahoe has seen boom times before, and the likes of Sergey Brin, Steve Jurvetson and others
have ignited another
. One resident described it as “the nicest San Francisco neighborhood.”
Listening:
“
The Interview: What Does Tucker Carlson Really Believe? I Went to Maine to Find Out
”
Reading:
“
Hated by All the Right People
” by Jason Zengerle
These days, the rules of media are as pliable as Play-Doh. And I’m embracing the fact here by breaking a tenet of this newsletter and combining these picks as a twofer: Maybe a little Tucker Carlson, the main subject of both selections, has rubbed off on me. He’s definitely a fella who has prospered by ignoring conventionalities.
The first thing to do here: Pick up “Hated by All the Right People” from Jason Zengerle, a longtime political correspondent. The biography of Carlson covers his ascension from traditional media wunderkind—contributor to places such as GQ, CNN and Tina Brown’s short-lived Talk magazine—to network TV provocateur, which is how most of us think of him today. Such was Carlson’s seeming ubiquity in the late 2010s that I’d forgotten how he toiled in the conservative hinterland until 2016, when Fox News greenlit his show just days before Donald Trump’s first election. Carlson’s ascension has run parallel to Trump’s, their intertwined rise mapping broadly to the tumultuous changes in America over the last 15-plus years.
Zengerle has great fun chronicling the absurd relationship between pontificator and president, with Carlson hyping up Trump on air and then kicking dirt all over his Brioni trousers in private, mocking his taste for gold, marble and sleaze. Off-air, they spoke together often, but sometimes Carlson liked to play mind games with Trump and would refuse to take his phone calls, which only made Trump intensify his pursuit of Carlson’s approval. One White House official likened their relationship to Trump as the eager suitor of Carlson, “a hot girl that didn’t want to fuck him.”
Zengerle has known Carlson since the ’90s, but Carlson wouldn’t speak to him for the book, and I did long to hear Carlson joust back—to hear what he has to say for himself. That’s why I suggest following “Hated by All the Right People,” which concludes as Trump wins reelection, with a recent episode of “The Interview,” the very good New York Times podcast, which ran two lengthy conversations with Carlson last week.
Right now, Carlson’s on-again, off-again relationship with Trump is very much off: He and Trump aren’t on speaking terms at all. Carlson is strictly opposed to the war in Iran, and the conflict has made him regret his decision to campaign for Trump in 2024, he told “The Interview.” By the time Carlson was done talking on the pod, I had fresh appreciation for Zengerle’s book. Yes, it’s a colorful portrait, but it’s also a study in concerted restraint. Clearly, Carlson is exactly how Zengerle describes him.
—Abram Brown
Watching:
“
Widow’s Bay
”
In retrospect, Mayor Larry Vaughn, the “Jaws” mayor who turned his community’s seascape into a great white’s smorgasbord, had it pretty easy. He only had one terrible creature to handle. By contrast, Tom Loftis (Matthew Rhys) has it much, much worse as mayor of Widow’s Bay, a fictional New England beach town: He has to deal with a killer ghost clown, a zombie woman, a lethal fog—and that’s just what’s made it on-screen in the first three episodes of Apple TV’s new horror comedy. The show has a dry, tense humor, and it is handled with great deftness by executive producer Hiro Murai, an “Atlanta” veteran, who directed most of the season’s 10 episodes. (Ti West, director of the popular “X” horror film trilogy, helmed one, too.)
As the series opens, Loftis has been working hard to boost Widow’s Bay tourist trade, which begins to pick up just as things increasingly go bump in the night. (Not that anyone was ever getting any sleep at the creepy haunted hotel even in the best of times.) Loftis even manages to get The New York Times to visit. The reporter ends up loving the place even though he accuses Loftis of trying to steal a little too much of Salem’s energy when he already has a perfectly good alternative to Martha’s Vineyard; the exchange comes just as Loftis insists everyone must stay indoors—the man-eating mist has returned.
Still, I know what element of Widow’s Bay would scare the bejeezus out of many of you. Absolutely no cellphone service. Anywhere.
—A.B.

标题: Billionaire Tax Refugees Flock to Ritzy Nevada Lake Town
内容:
Naveen Rao, a longtime California resident, ascended to a rarefied tier of wealth last year when his startup, Unconventional AI, was valued at $4.5 billion. The company is based in Palo Alto, but with the specter of a
new tax on billionaires
looming over the state, Rao began considering other places to live.
Last month, he found one: a $20 million mansion in Incline Village, a charming mountain town on the Nevada side of Lake Tahoe that has recently seen a fresh influx of the ultrawealthy. A substantial number of the newcomers are people like Rao who are trying to flee the California proposal or other wealth tax measures currently circulating around the country.
“Incline is the nicest San Francisco neighborhood,” Rao said, noting other recent tech arrivals in the area, including Google co-founder Sergey Brin, who reportedly also moved because of the tax, and SpaceX investor Steve Jurvetson. They join longtime Incline denizens such as Larry Ellison and Workday billionaire David Duffield, who relocated to the enclave many years ago.
Historically, the California side of the lake has held the cultural gravity—particularly the north and west sides, where Mark Zuckerberg currently has a place and where people like Dianne Feinstein have previously maintained homes. It’s a shorter drive to the Bay Area than from the Nevada side and closer to the region’s top ski destinations, like the former Alpine Meadows and Squaw Valley, now combined as Palisades.
Incline Village, which at 9,200 residents is the biggest Tahoeside settlement in Nevada, has attracted a steady stream of wealthy residents over the decades, many of them drawn to the state’s casinos and freewheeling ethos. Nevada’s lack of personal income tax has always been part of the draw, too—never more so than right now amid the AI boom and an expected wave of giant initial public offerings.
Real estate agents and other businesspeople in town say they are getting flooded with calls from people not only in California but also in New York, where lawmakers are
deliberating a handful of wealth tax proposals
amid a sense of momentum catalyzed by Zohran Mamdani’s mayorship. Washington state, which recently enacted a
yearly 10% millionaires’ tax
, is another source of new residents, they said.
“Our primary market is Californians seeking tax refuge in Nevada and people buying second homes,” said Cole Mizak, a top luxury real estate broker in Incline, who added that demand is higher than he’s seen in more than a decade in the area.
Christine Perry, a realtor who splits her time and work between Los Gatos, Calif., and Incline Village, said ultra–high-end properties have recently started moving much more quickly, driven by a recent exodus of the tech elite from Silicon Valley. Last year, three luxury estates of over $20 million sold in the town, including Brin’s purchase in December. But 2026 has already matched that total just four months into the year—a much faster clip than is typical for the small town.
The $42 million property Brin bought—a 5.14-acre estate with a guest quarters, beach house, two funiculars and a deep-water pier—had languished on the market for six years before he made his purchase.
“My buyers here in Nevada are high-net-worth individuals who are trying to position themselves here on the lake and get in early,” Perry said. “It’s like a scurry, and it’s coming from the wealth tax.”
Skiers at Palisades Tahoe ski resort in Olympic Valley, Calif.
(Getty Images)
A scan through local property records shows other recent sales in Incline and nearby on the lake’s Nevada side to entities linked to engineers at Nvidia and Anthropic, an OpenAI investor, and LLCs with addresses in places like Burlingame, Calif., and Seattle.
While the California billionaire tax proposal, which will be on the ballot in November, purports to backdate the tax to those who were living in the state on January 1, 2026,
many
law firms
have begun raising doubts about whether its retroactive provisions will stand up in court. Still, should the provision pass and survive a likely legal onslaught, the bill will come due at some point for those who reside in the state.
Rao said he is concerned the tax could kill his startup by forcing him to move shares despite his company’s early stage.
“I don’t have the value in cash required to pay it,” he said. “It’s not just a disincentive—my company will die or I’m insolvent.”
So he relocated to the area with his family at high speed this spring. They saw the house, closed on it within 10 days and had moved there before a month had passed.
Perry was the realtor who sold Rao the home: a 8,000-square-foot mansion with seven bedrooms and bathrooms, a two-story fireplace, and an eight-seat movie theater. Despite the breakneck pace of the purchase, Rao said he and his family were adjusting well to life in Tahoe.
“The mountains, the lake—you really can’t beat it,” Rao said. “Everything’s clean and pure. Just kind of the way it’s intended.”
He joins an increasingly plugged-in tech community in the area.
An LLC linked to Jurvetson closed on three properties in the area for a total of $178 million in February and March, including a $125 million mansion that was among the most expensive homes sold in the U.S. this year so far—and more than double the price of the most expensive property ever sold in Tahoe. (He couldn’t be reached for this story.)
Brin’s manse is just around the way, less than 1,000 feet from the California border. Meanwhile, Ellison purchased the 16-acre Hyatt resort in Incline Village in 2021 for $345 million.
It helps to have access to a private plane to connect life in a remote mountain getaway to cities like San Francisco and Palo Alto, where a majority of business is still done. Those with such good fortune can fly to the Bay Area in 40 minutes from a private terminal in nearby Truckee, rather than enduring the three-and-a-half hour journey by car. With a 25-minute drive to the Truckee airport from Incline Village, that’s not much more than a typical Bay Area commute from the suburbs.
When asked if there are enough techies to share planes to Bay Area meetings, Rao joked, “It’s sort of like, ‘You get one, I get the next.’”
Duffield, whose move to the area more than 25 years ago
touched off a yearslong legal battle
with California regulators over taxes, is a rare exception among the tech elite in making regular rounds around town, which have earned him bona fide local status, said Jennifer Tiexiera, a filmmaker who’s spent more than 20 years in Incline, having grown up there and moved back as a resident in 2019.
Tiexiera thinks many of the new tech elite in the area are less visible in the community than other wealthy residents of the area have been over the years, like junk bond king Michael Milken and casino magnate Steve Wynn.
“Milken put on our fireworks show every year. Those guys were like, ‘OK, we’re here!’” she said. “Now we joke, but no one’s even seen Larry Ellison. We know he bought everything, but that’s about it.”
She and her domestic partner, John Cheney, run three restaurants and a taco truck in the area while raising a six-year-old. Other factors have a much bigger impact on the local economy than the influx of the jet-setting class, such as the price of gas, winter snow levels and a shortage of affordable housing, she said—most workers at their restaurants commute 45 minutes from Reno.
Incline is also home to a younger crop of VCs, founders and investors who fall a notch or two below billionaire status, like Matthew Nordby, who runs a nine-figure VC fund, Flume Ventures, in the area with backing from Duffield and Scott McNealy, founder of Sun Microsystems. The group has seeded investments in dozens of companies, including Nevada-based AI chip company
Positron
and Nvidia cloud partner
Hydra Host
.
Nordby called Incline Village the “Hamptons of San Francisco” and said it wasn’t just a place for repose—the general clubbiness of the technorati has become part of the business draw.
“We don’t look at Incline as a place to retire,” Nordby said. “It’s rather an alternative to Sand Hill Road.”
A 1962 photo of the Cal-Neva lodge.
(Wikimedia Commons)
Furthermore, tax policies, regulations and other political issues in progressive states like California are increasingly driving more libertarian and right-wing techies to places like Nevada with more of an individualistic ethos.
“You have to understand Nevada is a frontier state of 3.5 million people. I can get the governor on the phone here,” he said. “Trying to navigate Sacramento is a lot more difficult—as everyone’s finding out.”
Nordby noted some of the area’s connections to Elon Musk as well. In the wake of the 2024
spat between the mercurial CEO and the state of Delaware
, Neuralink and X legally incorporated in Nevada. Musk’s cousin, Lyndon Rive, founder of solar company SolarCity, owns a lakeside mansion in Incline, where he
made waves in recent years
by proposing the construction of a three-story pool house for playing Octopush, an arcane game of underwater hockey. And Gigafactory Nevada, a giant Tesla battery and component factory, is a little more than an hour away.
McNealy is close to Musk, and there are a lot of other undisclosed SpaceX investors in the area who are well positioned for a windfall when the company does its IPO, Nordby said. “You have to be living under a rock not to see the SpaceX connections in Incline,” he said.
More amenities for the superelite seem to crop up every day.
A private school in the area, the Lake Tahoe School, has benefited from
the financial support
of people like Duffield and other luminaries, such as Beach Boys singer Mike Love, said Nordby, who sends his children there.
Still, there are some downsides to living in a paradise at 6,300 feet. The fire risk in Tahoe poses an omnipresent threat, as it does throughout the West.
Meanwhile, Chad McWhinney, CEO of Denver-based real estate firm Realberry and a friend of Elon’s brother, Kimbal Musk, is leading a group of investors, including
billionaire solar entrepreneur Hayes Barnard
, to rehab the historic Cal Neva resort nearby after purchasing it from Ellison in 2023.
Cal Neva, which has been shuttered for more than 12 years, was once owned by Frank Sinatra and has a series of underground tunnels once used to secretly shepherd around VIPs: John Kennedy, the Rat Pack, Marilyn Monroe and, legend has it, various mobsters.
McWhinney’s group is turning it into
a $600 million resort
in partnership with Santa Monica, Calif.–based Proper Hospitality that will feature a members-only club with yearly fees at over six figures a year at the highest tier, a lakeside pool, a 400-seat performance venue, a recording studio, a casino and 50,000 square feet of wellness space, all overseen by Los Angeles
design doyenne
Kelly Wearstler.
“It’s one of the most underserved luxury markets in the nation, if not the world,” McWhinney said. “Success often comes to those who get in front of the inevitable.”
And with a new crop of VIPs moving through the area, they’re definitely planning to keep the tunnels.
